vignette.text: A 68-year-old woman develops watery diarrhea 10 days after completing antibiotics for pneumonia. She has 8-10 stools daily.

physicalExam.abdomen: Mild diffuse tenderness, hyperactive bowel sounds

questions.question: First-line treatment for initial, non-severe CDI?
questions.explanation: IDSA 2021: Oral vancomycin 125mg QID or fidaxomicin 200mg BID x10 days. Metronidazole only if others unavailable.

platformLinks.text: 📚 C. difficile & Infectious Diarrhea Deep Dive

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected c. difficile & infectious diarrhea?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in c. difficile & infectious diarrhea. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of c. difficile & infectious diarrhea. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing c. difficile & infectious diarrhea, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

mcqs.stem: A 35-year-old with chronic diarrhea has anti-tissue transglutaminase (tTG) IgA of 120 U/mL (normal <20) and total IgA of 250 mg/dL. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Upper endoscopy with duodenal biopsies while patient remains on gluten-containing diet
options.explanation: Per ACG guidelines, positive tTG-IgA with adequate total IgA should be confirmed with duodenal biopsy showing villous atrophy (Marsh classification). Critically, the patient must remain on a gluten-containing diet — eliminating gluten before biopsy can cause false-negative histology.

options.text: Start a gluten-free diet immediately and recheck tTG in 6 months
options.explanation: Starting a GFD before biopsy confirmation can normalize histology, preventing definitive diagnosis. Biopsy is the gold standard and should be obtained first.

options.text: Check HLA-DQ2/DQ8 genotyping to confirm diagnosis
options.explanation: HLA-DQ2/DQ8 has high negative predictive value (absence essentially rules out celiac) but ~30% of the general population carries these alleles, so positive results alone do not confirm disease.

options.text: Repeat tTG-IgA in 3 months to confirm persistence
options.explanation: A tTG-IgA of 120 U/mL (6× upper limit) has very high positive predictive value. Repeating delays diagnosis unnecessarily; proceed to biopsy.
